why_correct: get_final_message() gives you the canonical Message object that the SDK assembles from the full stream — including server-computed input_tokens and output_tokens, not client-side estimates. The architectural principle is single-source-of-truth: the Message object is the authoritative artifact for both content and metadata, so you derive all downstream writes from it rather than reconstructing it piecemeal. Streaming and persistence are orthogonal concerns — stream for UX latency, capture the assembled object for durability and reporting.

distractors.A: The near-miss failure is token count accuracy. Client-side tokenizer libraries diverge from Anthropic's server-side tokenization, especially across model versions and special tokens — your cost-attribution dashboard will drift, sometimes materially. This approach would be acceptable if compliance logging were the only requirement and token counts weren't needed, but the stem explicitly requires accurate cost attribution, which rules it out.
distractors.C: This doubles your API spend and doubles latency on every turn to solve a problem the SDK already solves for free. The second call also returns a response to a different prompt context, so what you get back isn't the 'verified' version of the streamed reply — it's a new generation. In production this pattern breaks cost attribution entirely and introduces response divergence between what the agent read and what gets logged.
distractors.D: MessageDelta does carry usage data at stream end, but manually parsing event types and stitching them with an accumulated string is reinventing what get_final_message() already does. The failure mode is brittle event-parsing logic that breaks on SDK version bumps or edge cases like mid-stream errors — you're building a fragile reconstruction layer instead of consuming the SDK's assembly guarantee.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic here: stream for perception, assemble for persistence. Whenever you need token counts, structured metadata, or a durable record, the Message object from get_final_message() is the only safe source — never derive those from chunk accumulation or secondary calls. If the SDK gives you a first-class assembled artifact, treat chunk-level reconstruction as a code smell.
cross_domain_note: This pattern maps directly to cost governance: any cost-attribution system built on client-side token estimates instead of server-reported usage will fail audits and produce budget drift at scale — a common failure point in multi-tenant agentic deployments.

why_correct: When a compliance boundary exists between Claude and the end-user, streaming is a delivery optimization that must be subordinated to the trust boundary requirement — you cannot partially satisfy a redaction mandate. The correct pattern is server-side accumulation: buffer all ContentBlockDelta events into a complete response, apply full-document PII redaction and disclaimer injection atomically, then deliver. This trades TTFT latency for correctness, which is the only acceptable tradeoff when regulatory compliance is non-negotiable. The architectural principle is that streaming is an internal transport detail; the compliance scan is a gate that must operate on the complete artifact.

distractors.A: Increasing chunk size reduces the probability of boundary splits but never eliminates it — a 9-digit SSN can still straddle any fixed chunk boundary at the wrong offset. This is a probabilistic mitigation applied to a deterministic compliance requirement, which fails audit because 'unlikely' is not 'impossible.' A would be defensible only if the PII risk were acceptable below some frequency threshold, which compliance mandates never are.
distractors.C: The sliding-window overlap is the clever near-miss — it genuinely solves the boundary-spanning pattern problem for fixed-width PII like SSNs. The failure mode is that it adds stateful complexity to the streaming path, still requires careful overlap sizing for variable-length patterns, and still cannot handle the disclaimer-appending requirement without knowing the response is complete. It solves one part of the problem while leaving the disclaimer injection architecturally unresolved, making B strictly superior in scope.
distractors.D: Using a secondary Claude call per chunk to scan for PII is the wrong tool at the wrong granularity — a Claude model has non-deterministic latency, adds per-chunk cost, and introduces the same boundary-spanning vulnerability that broke the regex approach. You've also now created an agentic loop in the hot path of a compliance gate, which is a latency and reliability anti-pattern.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: whenever a compliance or trust-boundary gate must be satisfied before user delivery, streaming collapses to buffering — TTFT is a UX preference, not a compliance constraint. Any architecture that lets unvalidated tokens cross a trust boundary in pursuit of latency has inverted the priority order.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to the prompt injection trust boundary domain: just as you never let unvetted tool output re-enter the orchestration layer without sanitization, you never let unvetted model output cross into the user trust zone without full validation — the streaming context just makes the violation less obvious.

why_correct: The core issue is that ContentBlockDelta events are arbitrary byte-boundary slices of the token stream — the streaming protocol makes no guarantee that a semantic unit like 'user@example.com' arrives within a single delta. A regex applied per-chunk operates on fragments, not tokens, so a split like 'user@ex' / 'ample.com' silently evades the match. The correct pattern is buffer-then-process: accumulate all deltas into a complete document, apply stateful transformations (redaction, header injection) against the full string, then forward the sanitized output. This trades live-streaming UX for correctness guarantees that the compliance requirement mandates.

distractors.A: The failure mode here isn't latency — it's correctness. Async redaction in a parallel thread still operates on individual delta payloads, so the split-token problem persists; you've just moved the broken regex off the hot path without fixing the fundamental boundary mismatch. A is right if the constraint is throughput optimization on a regex that's provably safe to apply per-chunk; that's not the constraint here.
distractors.C: Pushing redaction to the React client moves the trust boundary to an untrusted surface — this is a compliance anti-pattern, not a performance win. Institutional policy requiring redaction before display means the server is the enforcement point; client-side redaction is trivially bypassed, fails audit, and exposes PII in transit to the browser regardless of what the UI renders.
distractors.D: Switching to synchronous messages.create() does give you a fully-formed response object, but it's an overcorrection that destroys streaming infrastructure for all other use cases. The correct remediation is accumulate-then-forward at the server layer, not a protocol downgrade — you preserve the streaming pipeline for cases that don't require post-processing.

mental_model: The decision rule: any transformation that requires semantic completeness (regex over structured data, header injection, content policy checks) cannot be applied at the delta level — deltas are a transport artifact, not a semantic unit. When post-processing requires full-document context, the correct architecture is always server-side accumulation before client delivery, and you accept the UX tradeoff explicitly.
cross_domain_note: This boundary-split failure is the streaming equivalent of chunked RAG retrieval — just as you wouldn't run entity extraction on mid-sentence embedding chunks, you can't run structured redaction on sub-token stream slices. Both require semantic completeness before transformation.
